title: Global Market Update
summary:
Main
By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
Rising concerns that the rising Covid infection rates will lead to new restrictions and lockdowns in China have put pressure on the Chinese financial markets, as investors are pricing in renewed disruptions to the country's economy. A new round of lockdowns could potentially derail the fragile recovery of the world's second-largest economy and dim the demand outlook for many commodities. In addition, the increasingly precarious state of the global economy will mean any weakness in the Chinese economy will add fuel to the recession narrative.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: Rising concerns that the rising Covid infection rates will lead to new restrictions and lockdowns in China have put pressure on the Chinese financial markets, as investors are pricing in renewed disruptions to the country's economy. A new round of lockdowns could potentially derail the fragile recovery of the world's second-largest economy and dim the demand outlook for many commodities.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: Oil prices took a severe beating during yesterday's trading session amid increasing fears that the global economy will fall into a recession and increasing risks of new Covid restrictions in China, the world's largest importer of the commodity. Brent futures dipped below 100 dollars per barrel to a three-month low following a 7.1 per cent decline.

children.title: Freight Markets
children.summary: Dry bulk freight rates retreated yesterday, with all of the Baltic Exchange's indices in the red. In contrast to recent days, the Capesizes returned to the role as the worst performer, and the segment's sub-index declined by 4.9 per cent.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: Thermal coal prices have been steadily gaining ground in the last month, with Newcastle futures trading at record highs. Tight global supplies and a revival of the demand for the dirtiest of fossil fuels amid disruptions and high prices in the natural gas markets have been the primary drivers behind the recent gains.
